pattern: Order tasks by how much slack energy they demand beyond what they actually consume, then greedily top up a running energy total only when a task's minimum requirement isn't already met.
hints: Doing tasks in an arbitrary order can force you to carry more starting energy than necessary — the order in which you attempt tasks changes the answer.
A task with a big gap between its minimum requirement and its actual cost should be done while your energy is highest, so sort tasks by (minimum - actual) descending.

Walk the sorted tasks keeping a running energy total; whenever it's below a task's minimum, record how much extra you'd have needed and top the running total up to that minimum, then subtract the actual cost — the sum of all top-ups is the answer.

edge_inputs.description: single task, needed energy equals its own minimum
every task has zero slack, actual equals minimum for all of them
one task demands far more minimum energy than the rest combined
tasks already given in the order the greedy would pick them
tasks given in the opposite order the greedy would pick them
tasks sit at the value extremes, actual = 1 and minimum = 10000
two tasks tie on slack, ordering between equal-slack tasks must not change the total

stress.description: many tasks with random actual/minimum pairs and random slack, in random input order
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    tasks = []
    for _ in range(n):
        actual = rng.randint(1, 10000)
        minimum = rng.randint(actual, 10000)
        tasks.append([actual, minimum])
    rng.shuffle(tasks)
    return [tasks]
stress.note: minimum is always sampled >= actual to respect the constraint that minimum requirement never falls below the task's own cost
